This article is about the men's team. For the women's team, see [Wales women's national rugby sevens team](/wiki/Wales_women%27s_national_rugby_sevens_team "Wales women's national rugby sevens team").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/40/Men%27s_rugby_sevens_at_the_2022_Commonwealth_Games_-_Wales_vs_Malaysia_183610.jpg/330px-Men%27s_rugby_sevens_at_the_2022_Commonwealth_Games_-_Wales_vs_Malaysia_183610.jpg)](/wiki/File:Men%27s_rugby_sevens_at_the_2022_Commonwealth_Games_-_Wales_vs_Malaysia_183610.jpg)

Team Wales at the [2022 Commonwealth Games](/wiki/Rugby_sevens_at_the_2022_Commonwealth_Games "Rugby sevens at the 2022 Commonwealth Games").

The **Wales national rugby sevens team** did compete at the annual [World Rugby Sevens Series](/wiki/SVNS "SVNS") between 2000 and 2022,[[1]](#cite_note-bbc-1)[[2]](#cite_note-scot-2) however merged with [England](/wiki/England_national_rugby_sevens_team "England national rugby sevens team") and [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland_national_rugby_sevens_team "Scotland national rugby sevens team") to form the [Great Britain sevens team](/wiki/Great_Britain_national_rugby_sevens_team "Great Britain national rugby sevens team") from the [2022–23](/wiki/2022%E2%80%9323_World_Rugby_Sevens_Series "2022–23 World Rugby Sevens Series") season onward.[[1]](#cite_note-bbc-1)[[2]](#cite_note-scot-2) The team also competes at the quadrennial [Rugby World Cup Sevens](/wiki/Rugby_World_Cup_Sevens "Rugby World Cup Sevens") and the [Commonwealth Games](/wiki/Rugby_sevens_at_the_Commonwealth_Games "Rugby sevens at the Commonwealth Games").[[1]](#cite_note-bbc-1)[[2]](#cite_note-scot-2)

[Wales](/wiki/Wales "Wales") were the World Cup Sevens Champions after winning the [2009 Rugby World Cup Sevens](/wiki/2009_Rugby_World_Cup_Sevens "2009 Rugby World Cup Sevens") in the [United Arab Emirates](/wiki/United_Arab_Emirates "United Arab Emirates"). But they relinquished that title after failing to defend their crown at the [2013 Rugby World Cup Sevens](/wiki/2013_Rugby_World_Cup_Sevens "2013 Rugby World Cup Sevens") in [Moscow](/wiki/Moscow "Moscow").

The Welsh sevens squad was disbanded by the [Welsh Rugby Union](/wiki/Welsh_Rugby_Union "Welsh Rugby Union") (WRU) due to financial constraints. After a three-year absence, the Wales sevens team returned to international competition in the [2006–07](/wiki/2006%E2%80%9307_IRB_Sevens_World_Series "2006–07 IRB Sevens World Series") season.[[3]](#cite_note-3) They competed at half of the eight tournaments and won the plate competition (fifth place) at each of them.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] They repeated this feat at the [2006 Commonwealth Games](/wiki/Rugby_Sevens_at_the_2006_Commonwealth_Games "Rugby Sevens at the 2006 Commonwealth Games").

The star of the 2005–06 squad was [Neath RFC](/wiki/Neath_RFC "Neath RFC") and [Ospreys](/wiki/Ospreys_(rugby_union) "Ospreys (rugby union)") player [James Hook](/wiki/James_Hook_(rugby_union) "James Hook (rugby union)"). Hook later progressed to the [Wales national 15-a-side team](/wiki/Wales_national_rugby_union_team "Wales national rugby union team"). In 2006–07, Wales competed in the Dubai, South Africa, Australia, Hong Kong, Scotland and England legs of the [IRB](/wiki/International_Rugby_Board "International Rugby Board")'s [World Sevens Series](/wiki/World_Sevens_Series "World Sevens Series"), reaching the semi-finals of the cup at [Twickenham](/wiki/Twickenham_Stadium "Twickenham Stadium") and [Murrayfield Stadium](/wiki/Murrayfield_Stadium "Murrayfield Stadium"). Wales have been a core team that has competed in all legs of the IRB Sevens Series since the [2007–08 season](/wiki/2007%E2%80%9308_IRB_Sevens_World_Series "2007–08 IRB Sevens World Series").

At the [2016 USA Sevens](/wiki/2016_USA_Sevens "2016 USA Sevens"), Wales beat Canada, Portugal, Scotland and France to win the Bowl final and claim 9th place.

## 2009 Rugby World Cup Sevens

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wales_national_rugby_sevens_team&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 2009 Rugby World Cup Sevens")]

Main article: [2009 Rugby World Cup Sevens](/wiki/2009_Rugby_World_Cup_Sevens "2009 Rugby World Cup Sevens")

In Wales's first ever cup final appearance in a major rugby sevens event, Wales played Argentina in the 2009 World Cup Final.

In the Group stages Wales beat Zimbabwe 31–5 and Uruguay 27–0 before losing to Argentina 14–0 in the final pool match, leaving Wales uncertain of a cup quarterfinals spot. With results going their way Wales made it to the cup quarterfinals as one of the second place qualifiers for the first time in their history.

Wales beat favourites New Zealand in the quarterfinals 15–14, and defeated Samoa in the semifinals 19–12.

Wales faced Argentina for the second time in the tournament in the Final. Wales started with the same team that played against New Zealand and Samoa earlier in the day. At half time Wales had a lead of 12–7 after tries from [Richie Pugh](/wiki/Richie_Pugh "Richie Pugh") and [Tal Selley](/wiki/Tal_Selley "Tal Selley").
In the second half Argentina levelled the score at 12–12. With less than 90 seconds left, Wales's [Aled Thomas](/wiki/Aled_Thomas "Aled Thomas") scored underneath the posts and with a successful conversion put Wales into the lead at 19–12.
Argentina claimed the restart. After the siren sounded to indicate there was no time left on the clock Argentina fumbled the ball in a ruck leading to the ball being kicked out of play and Wales being crowned the 2009 Sevens Rugby World Cup Champions.
